references.citation: Mubarak, S., Habib, M. A., & Zuhairi, M. F. (2021). Anomaly Detection in ICS Datasets with Machine Learning Algorithms. Computer Systems Science & Engineering, 37(1). https://doi.org/10.32604/csse.2021.014384
references.title: Anomaly Detection in ICS Datasets with Machine Learning Algorithms
references.authors: Mubarak, S., Habib, M. A., & Zuhairi, M. F.
references.abstract: Evaluates ML algorithms for anomaly detection on ICS datasets, finding logistic regression achieves ~99.99% accuracy while noting dataset bias as a key limitation for real-world deployment.
key_findings: Logistic regression achieved ~99.99% accuracy on ICS dataset
Supervised ML outperforms rule-based detection
Flow-based features effective for ICS anomaly classification
Dataset bias noted as limitation requiring real-world validation
references.relevance: Directly validates the ML detection approach used in the prototype and provides baseline accuracy benchmarks for ICS anomaly classification. The dataset bias limitation informs multi-dataset evaluation strategy.

references.citation: Segal, M., Avin, C., & Scalosub, G. (2021). SOAR: Minimizing network utilization with bounded in-network computing. In Proceedings of the 20th ACM Workshop on Hot Topics in Networks (HotNets '21). https://doi.org/10.1145/3485983.3494853
references.title: SOAR: Minimizing network utilization with bounded in-network computing
references.authors: Segal, M., Avin, C., & Scalosub, G.
references.abstract: Presents the SOAR framework for minimizing network utilization through bounded in-network aggregation, with peer-reviewed empirical evaluation of throughput improvements in distributed environments.
key_findings: SOAR framework reduces network utilization through in-network aggregation
Bounded aggregation improves throughput measurably
Peer-reviewed empirical evaluation
Applicable to distributed security operations
references.relevance: Provides the foundational SOAR architecture concept that underpins the ML-SOAR prototype, offering a validated model for efficient distributed processing relevant to SOC automation workflows.

references.citation: Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency. (2025). Foundations for OT cybersecurity: Asset inventory guidance. U.S. Department of Homeland Security. https://www.cisa.gov/sites/default/files/2025-08/joint-guide-foundations-for-OT-cybersecurity-asset-inventory-guidance_508c.pdf
references.title: Foundations for OT Cybersecurity: Asset Inventory Guidance
references.abstract: CISA/NSA joint publication establishing standardized OT asset inventory methodology with cross-sector taxonomy and lifecycle management frameworks for ICS/OT devices.
key_findings: Standardized OT asset inventory methodology
Cross-sector taxonomy for ICS/OT assets
Lifecycle management framework for OT devices
CISA/NSA joint publication with federal authority
references.relevance: Provides authoritative federal guidance for OT asset classification and inventory practices that directly inform the threat categorization and system mapping within the prototype's dataset structure.

references.citation: National Institute of Standards and Technology. (2022). Guide to Industrial Control Systems (ICS) Security (NIST Special Publication 800-82 Rev. 3). https://doi.org/10.6028/NIST.SP.800-82r3
references.title: Guide to Industrial Control Systems (ICS) Security (SP 800-82 Rev. 3)
references.authors: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
references.abstract: Globally recognized federal baseline for ICS/OT security covering layered defense architecture, IT/OT network segmentation, and incident response procedures for cyber-physical systems.
key_findings: Layered defense architecture for ICS environments
Network segmentation requirements for IT/OT boundary
Incident response procedures for cyber-physical systems
Globally recognized federal baseline standard
references.relevance: Establishes the IT/OT security framework and Purdue model segmentation principles that structure the threat taxonomy and layer classifications throughout the prototype dashboard and datasets.

references.citation: Merlano, J. C. (2024). Enhancing Cyber Security through AI and ML: A Literature Review. Journal of Cyber Security, 6(1). https://www.techscience.com/JCS/v6n1/58880/html
references.title: Enhancing Cyber Security through AI and ML: A Literature Review
references.abstract: Systematic review of 90 studies across 5 databases (2015-2024) analyzing ML applications in cybersecurity, finding threat detection/prevention as the dominant research category at 27.2% of publications.
key_findings: 90 studies analyzed across 5 databases (2015-2024)
Threat detection/prevention = 27.2% of publications — largest research category
Primary ML applications: anomaly detection, IDS, malware classification, phishing detection
LIME/SHAP enable explainable AI for cybersecurity models
Data quality and black-box interpretability are primary barriers
references.relevance: Situates the prototype's ML-SOAR approach within the broader cybersecurity literature landscape and validates explainability (LIME/SHAP) as a research priority for production-ready ML detection systems.

references.citation: Architecture Technology Corporation. (2022). AI Modeling for Cyber-Attack Simulation (U.S. Patent No. 11429713B1). U.S. Patent and Trademark Office. https://patents.google.com/patent/US11429713B1/en
references.title: AI Modeling for Cyber-Attack Simulation (U.S. Patent No. 11429713B1)
references.abstract: Patented system for adaptive AI-driven cyber-attack simulation using dual neural network and expert decision tree evaluation, operating autonomously after initial exercise condition setup.
key_findings: Adaptive AI-driven cyber-attack simulation without pre-scripted scenarios
Dual evaluation using neural networks + expert decision trees
System functions autonomously after initial exercise conditions set
Real-time monitoring of both active and passive data during exercises
references.relevance: Demonstrates patented prior art for AI-driven threat simulation relevant to validating the prototype's ML-based attack scenario modeling and automated response decision logic.

references.citation: Nozomi Networks. (2024). How Is AI Used in OT/ICS Cybersecurity? Nozomi Networks. https://www.nozominetworks.com/cybersecurity-faqs/how-is-ai-used-in-ot-ics-cybersecurity
references.title: How Is AI Used in OT/ICS Cybersecurity?
references.abstract: Industry practitioner analysis of AI/ML deployment in OT/ICS environments, documenting 50-70% accuracy improvements in asset intelligence and SOC efficiency gains from behavioral baselining.
key_findings: Behavioral baselining detects zero-day exploits beyond signature methods
Asset intelligence 50-70% more accurate with ML vs. manual classification
SOC efficiency: week-long tasks reduced to daily single-analyst work
Adaptive learning filters benign deviations while flagging true anomalies
references.relevance: Provides commercial validation of the prototype's core value proposition — ML-driven SOC efficiency gains in OT/ICS environments — with quantified operational metrics from production deployments.

references.citation: Matoušek, P., Ryšavý, O., & Grégr, M. (2022). ICS Dataset for Smart Grid Anomaly Detection [Dataset]. IEEE DataPort. https://doi.org/10.21227/1trw-n685
references.title: ICS Dataset for Smart Grid Anomaly Detection
references.authors: Matoušek, P., Ryšavý, O., & Grégr, M.
references.abstract: IEEE DataPort dataset containing IEC 60870-104 and IEC 61850 MMS smart grid protocol traffic with attack scenarios including scanning, injection, and DoS; 113.2 MB CC-BY-4.0 licensed.
key_findings: IEC 60870-104 and IEC 61850 MMS protocol traffic in CSV format
Attack scenarios: scanning, switching commands, injection, DoS, rogue devices
113.2 MB dataset with PCAP source files
CC-BY-4.0 licensed
references.relevance: Provides OT-layer smart grid protocol traffic with labeled attack scenarios, extending the prototype's dataset coverage to ICS-specific protocols beyond the IT-focused EDGE-IIoTset and CIC-IDS2017 datasets.

references.citation: Sharafaldin, I., Lashkari, A. H., & Ghorbani, A. A. (2018). Toward Generating a New Intrusion Detection Dataset and Intrusion Traffic Characterization. In Proceedings of ICISSP 2018. University of New Brunswick Canadian Institute for Cybersecurity. https://www.unb.ca/cic/datasets/ids-2017.html
references.title: Intrusion Detection Evaluation Dataset (CIC-IDS2017)
references.authors: Sharafaldin, I., Lashkari, A. H., & Ghorbani, A. A.
references.abstract: UNB benchmark IDS dataset with 5 days of realistic traffic from 25-user behavioral profiles, covering 8 attack types and 80+ network flow features across 51.1 GB, addressing 11 benchmark gaps in prior datasets.
key_findings: 5 days of realistic traffic (July 3-7 2017) with 25-user behavioral profiles
8 attack types: brute force, DoS, Heartbleed, web attacks, infiltration, botnet, DDoS
51.1 GB with 80+ network flow features in CSV
Addresses 11 benchmark criteria missing from prior datasets
references.relevance: One of the most widely cited IDS benchmark datasets; included in the prototype to enable cross-dataset generalizability testing of the ML-SOAR detection pipeline against a ground-truth labeled IT traffic benchmark.

references.citation: AI in Cybersecurity: Detecting and Preventing Cyber Threats using Machine Learning. Clareus. https://clareus.org/pdf/csse/CSSE-02-051.pdf
references.title: AI in Cybersecurity: Detecting and Preventing Cyber Threats using Machine Learning
references.abstract: Comparative study demonstrating ML algorithm superiority over signature-based detection, with ensemble methods reducing false positives in IDS deployments and deep learning achieving real-time classification accuracy.
key_findings: ML algorithms significantly outperform traditional signature-based detection
Deep learning models achieve high accuracy in real-time threat classification
Ensemble methods reduce false positive rates in IDS deployments
Supervised and unsupervised methods complement each other for comprehensive coverage
references.relevance: Supports the prototype's dual-layer detection strategy combining supervised classification with unsupervised anomaly detection to minimize false positives in OT environments where alert fatigue is critical.

references.citation: AI and Machine Learning in Cyber Threat Detection. ResearchGate. https://www.researchgate.net/publication/383693454_AI_AND_MACHINE_LEARNING_IN_CYBER_THREAT_DETECTION
references.title: AI and Machine Learning in Cyber Threat Detection
references.abstract: Survey of ML approaches to cyber threat detection covering feature engineering, transfer learning, and real-time inference optimization for production detection pipelines.
key_findings: Survey of current ML approaches to cyber threat detection
Feature engineering critical for effective threat classification
Transfer learning reduces training data requirements
Real-time detection requires optimized model inference pipelines
references.relevance: Feature engineering and transfer learning findings directly inform the prototype's approach to limited labeled OT dataset availability, enabling effective detection with smaller domain-specific training sets.

references.citation: Neumann, P. (n.d.). Cyber Threat Analysis using AI and Machine Learning [White paper]. Softrams. https://p-neumann.github.io/trustworthyai/doc/wp_Softrams_p1_predictive_analysis_ML.pdf
references.title: Cyber Threat Analysis using AI and Machine Learning
references.abstract: Industry whitepaper on predictive ML threat analysis, covering behavioral analytics, NLP-driven threat intelligence correlation, and automated response workflows for SOC efficiency.
key_findings: Predictive analysis using ML enables proactive threat identification
Behavioral analytics reduce mean time to detection
NLP applied to threat intelligence feeds improves alert correlation
Automated response workflows reduce analyst fatigue
references.relevance: NLP-driven threat intelligence correlation and automated response workflows align directly with the SOAR automation layer of the prototype, supporting the MTTD/MTTR reduction hypothesis.

references.citation: IEEE International Network Generations Roadmap. (2022). AI/ML Chapter (2022 Edition). IEEE Future Networks. https://futurenetworks.ieee.org/images/files/pdf/INGR-2022-Edition/IEEE_INGR_AIML_Chapter_2022-Edition-FINAL.pdf
references.title: IEEE INGR AI/ML Chapter (2022 Edition)
references.abstract: IEEE framework for AI/ML integration in network security with standardized evaluation metrics, federated learning approaches for privacy-preserving detection, and edge AI latency reduction guidance.
key_findings: IEEE framework for AI/ML integration in network security
Standardized evaluation metrics for ML-based detection systems
Federated learning approaches for privacy-preserving threat detection
Edge AI reduces latency for real-time OT threat response
references.relevance: IEEE standardized evaluation metrics provide the benchmarking framework for comparing prototype detection performance across datasets, ensuring results are reproducible and comparable to peer research.

references.citation: International Journal of Innovative Research and Technology. ML-Based Network Intrusion Detection: Comparative Analysis. IJIRT. https://ijirt.org/publishedpaper/IJIRT187408_PAPER.pdf
references.title: ML-Based Network Intrusion Detection: Comparative Analysis
references.abstract: Comparative analysis of ML algorithms for network intrusion detection, validating random forest and gradient boosting performance with feature selection to reduce computational overhead.
key_findings: Comparative analysis of ML algorithms for network intrusion detection
Random forest and gradient boosting show strong IDS performance
Feature selection reduces computational overhead without accuracy loss
Real-world dataset validation confirms lab results
references.relevance: Algorithm comparison methodology and feature selection findings guide the prototype's model selection process, particularly for edge-deployable lightweight detection in resource-constrained OT environments.

references.citation: World Journal of Advanced Research and Reviews. (2024). Recent Advances in AI-Driven Cybersecurity. WJARR, 21(1). https://doi.org/10.30574/wjarr.2024.21.1.0287
references.title: Recent Advances in AI-Driven Cybersecurity
references.authors: World Journal of Advanced Research and Reviews
references.abstract: Review of recent advances in AI-driven cybersecurity, finding hybrid ML approaches outperform single-algorithm detection and highlighting SIEM/SOAR integration challenges.
key_findings: Recent advances in AI-driven cybersecurity reviewed
Hybrid ML approaches outperform single-algorithm detection
Explainability requirements shape model selection in security contexts
Integration challenges between ML systems and legacy SIEM/SOAR platforms
references.relevance: SIEM/SOAR integration challenges identified here directly inform the prototype's architecture design decisions, particularly the API-first approach for compatibility with existing SOC toolchains.

references.citation: Security of ML-Based Anomaly Detection in Cyber-Physical Systems. (2022). IEEE Xplore. https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/9868845
references.title: Security of ML-Based Anomaly Detection in Cyber-Physical Systems
references.abstract: Analysis of adversarial attack impacts on ML anomaly detection in CPS environments, demonstrating up to 40% accuracy degradation and proposing defense strategies including adversarial training and model ensembling.
key_findings: Adversarial attacks can degrade ML anomaly detection accuracy by up to 40%
Defense strategies include adversarial training and model ensembling
CPS-specific threats require domain-aware ML model design
Physical process constraints can be encoded as detection features
references.relevance: Adversarial robustness findings are critical for the prototype's threat model, informing the need for ensemble-based detection and physical process constraint encoding in the OT-layer anomaly detection module.

references.citation: Springer. (2025). AI and machine learning in cybersecurity: State-of-the-art techniques. Knowledge and Information Systems. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10115-025-02429-y
references.title: AI and Machine Learning in Cybersecurity: State-of-the-Art Techniques
references.abstract: Comprehensive 2025 survey of deep learning for cybersecurity covering transformer models for log analysis, reinforcement learning for automated incident response, and quantum-resistant detection approaches.
key_findings: Comprehensive survey of deep learning for cybersecurity through 2025
Transformer models emerging as strong performers for log analysis
Reinforcement learning applied to automated incident response
Quantum-resistant approaches under active research
references.relevance: Transformer and reinforcement learning findings establish the forward-looking research direction for the prototype, positioning the ML-SOAR approach within the current state of the art and identifying next-phase improvements.

references.title: Foundations for OT Cybersecurity: Asset Inventory Guidance for Owners and Operators
references.authors: CISA, EPA, NSA, FBI, and International Partners
references.citation: CISA et al. (2025). Foundations for OT Cybersecurity: Asset Inventory Guidance. Joint Guidance Document.
references.key_findings: Joint guidance establishing asset inventory as a foundational OT security control. Recommends passive discovery methods to avoid disrupting ICS/OT processes. Emphasizes knowing what devices exist on the network before applying detection or response measures. Directly supports ML-SOAR asset-aware detection pipelines.
references.relevance: Provides authoritative baseline for asset enumeration that feeds ML anomaly detection — you cannot detect deviations without knowing the baseline asset inventory. Supports the data collection phase of the ML-SOAR pipeline.

references.title: Authorities Disrupt World's Largest IoT DDoS Botnets (AISURU, Kimwolf, JackSkid, Mossad)
references.authors: U.S. Department of Justice, USAO-AK, Canadian and German Law Enforcement
references.citation: DOJ (2026). Authorities Disrupt World's Largest IoT DDoS Botnets Responsible for Record-Breaking Attacks. DOJ Press Release, March 20, 2026.
references.key_findings: Four IoT botnets (AISURU, Kimwolf, JackSkid, Mossad) infected 3M+ devices globally. Record 31.4 Tbps DDoS attack in November 2025. Kimwolf issued 1.7 billion commands in 4 days. DoD networks targeted. C2 infrastructure comprised ~1,000 servers; Lumen null-routed them in coordination with 15 tech firms.
references.relevance: Demonstrates real-world scale of IoT-based DDoS threats that intersect with CPS environments. Botnet techniques (ADB exploitation, EtherHiding, DNS-over-TLS evasion) are directly relevant to OT network anomaly detection research and ML-SOAR detection pipeline design.

references.title: Predictive Analysis Using Machine Learning for Cybersecurity
references.citation: Softrams. (2024). Predictive Analysis Using Machine Learning for Cybersecurity. Trustworthy AI Working Paper Series.
references.key_findings: Examines ML-based predictive models for proactive cybersecurity threat detection. Covers supervised and unsupervised approaches for anomaly detection in networked systems. Addresses challenges of model explainability in security operations contexts.
references.relevance: Directly supports the predictive detection component of the ML-SOAR framework. Provides methodological grounding for applying ML classification models to cyber threat prediction in IT/OT environments.

references.title: Cybersecurity in Industrial Control Systems: Challenges and Solutions
references.authors: Computer Science and Software Engineering (CSSE) Journal
references.citation: CSSE. (2024). Cybersecurity in Industrial Control Systems: Challenges and Solutions. CSSE-02-051.
references.key_findings: Surveys security vulnerabilities specific to ICS/SCADA systems including protocol weaknesses (Modbus, DNP3). Proposes defense-in-depth strategies aligned with Purdue Reference Model. Discusses detection limitations of signature-based approaches in OT environments.
references.relevance: Provides academic grounding for ICS-specific security challenges addressed by the ML-SOAR prototype. The protocol weakness analysis informs feature engineering for the ML detection pipeline.
